name: Great Depression Crash 1929–1933
primary_causes: Margin buying (10:1 leverage) on overvalued stocks
Bank failures (~9,000 banks, 1930–33)
Fed raised rates in 1931 to defend gold standard
Smoot-Hawley Tariff caused global trade collapse (-66%)
Debt deflation spiral
No deposit insurance — bank runs
entry_signals: Market up 350%+ in 5 years
Record margin debt relative to GDP
Economic indicators peaked August 1929
Fed tightening cycle in progress
key_lesson: Bank system integrity is the foundation — let banks fail and you get depression. Central bank passivity in a deflationary spiral is catastrophic.

name: Black Monday 1987
primary_causes: Portfolio insurance (automated selling cascade)
10-year yields surged from 7.5% to 10.2%
Overvaluation (250% rise in 5 years, P/E elevated)
Trade deficit shock announcement (Oct 14)
Program trading amplification
No circuit breakers existed
entry_signals: Yields rising sharply for 3+ months
P/E ratios elevated
Market up 250% without major correction
Rising inflation concerns
recovery_driver: Fed liquidity injection; no recession followed; Greenspan Put established
key_lesson: Leverage/derivatives can create mechanical cascades that overshoot fundamentals. The absence of recession means recovery can be swift.

name: Dot-com Crash 2000–2002
primary_causes: Extreme valuation: NASDAQ P/E infinite (no earnings), S&P CAPE >44x
Fed tightening: 300bp in 12 months ending June 2000
Revenue/earnings fiction in many dot-coms
Y2K tech overspend cut sharply in 2000
Accounting fraud: Enron, WorldCom ($11B fraud)
Capital misallocation: $1T+ invested in companies with no viable model
Lock-up expiry selling: Insiders dumped billions at peak
entry_signals: CAPE ratio >44x (all-time high)
457 internet IPOs in 1999–2000
Margin debt record: $278B (March 2000)
Fed tightening cycle in progress (6 hikes)
Yield curve inverting
Early-stage dot-coms already failing before index peaked
First-wave IPOs trading below offering price by late 1999
key_lesson: Real earnings matter. When asset prices are priced for perfection with no earnings, any deceleration in narrative destroys valuation instantly. Recovery from valuation excess is very slow because P/E must reset.

name: Global Financial Crisis 2007–2009
primary_causes: Housing bubble: prices +124% 1997–2006 on fraudulent lending
Subprime mortgage origination: $100B (2001) → $600B (2006)
CDO/MBS securitization hid risk and distributed it globally
Investment bank leverage: 30–40:1 (Bear Stearns, Lehman)
Rating agencies AAA-rated toxic instruments (conflict of interest)
CDS market: $60T+ in credit default swap exposure with no collateral
Correlation assumptions in models catastrophically wrong
'Originate to distribute' removed incentive for loan quality

timeline_key_events.event: Two Bear Stearns hedge funds fail
BNP Paribas freezes three funds; interbank lending freezes
Bear Stearns collapses; Fed arranges JPMorgan rescue
Money market funds break the buck

entry_signals: ABX subprime MBS index declining from early 2007
Yield curve inverted 2006–2007
HY spreads at extreme tight (complacency) in 2006
Home price appreciation slowing mid-2006
Subprime delinquencies rising Q3 2006
Bear Stearns hedge fund stress March 2007 (6 months early)
LIBOR-OIS spread blowing out August 2007
what_worked_as_hedge: Short financials (GS, C, BAC, LEH pre-collapse)
Short MBS via CDS (the trade in The Big Short)

policy_response.fed_actions: Cut FFR 5.25% → 0%; QE1/QE2/QE3 total ~$3.5T; unlimited forward guidance
policy_response.regulatory: Dodd-Frank 2010; stress tests; Basel III capital requirements

key_lesson: Hidden leverage + correlated risk across the entire financial system can make what looks like a housing correction into a global financial meltdown. The bigger the leverage, the deeper the crash.

name: COVID Crash 2020
primary_causes: COVID-19 global pandemic — unexpected external shock
Global economic lockdowns: ~4B people quarantined
Oil price war: Saudi Arabia vs Russia (Brent crude -75%)
Credit market freeze: HY spreads 1,100bp in days
Treasury market dysfunction (even Treasuries had liquidity issues)
Uncertainty about pandemic duration and economic recovery
entry_signals: No classical economic signals — pure external shock
COVID cases rising in China from January 2020 (market ignored)
Diamond Princess ship cluster early February
VIX was 13 (extreme complacency) day before crash began
CAPE ratio 30x+ at peak (elevated but not sole cause)
what_worked_as_hedge: Cash briefly; then missing recovery was costly

policy_response.fed: 0% rates + $120B/month QE + corporate bond buying + all 2008 facilities restarted within days
policy_response.fiscal: CARES Act $2.2T + $900B + ARP $1.9T = ~$5.5T total US fiscal

recovery_explanation: Fastest recovery because: (1) shock was external not structural; (2) policy response was instantaneous and record-scale; (3) vaccine solved root cause in 11 months; (4) digital economy thrived under lockdowns
key_lesson: Unpredictable shocks to structurally sound systems recover fastest when policy responds immediately and at scale. The crash was about fear, not fundamentals — once fear resolved, assets repriced sharply higher.

name: AI Boom 2023–Present
drivers: ChatGPT sparked mass awareness of commercial AI viability
NVIDIA data center revenue grew ~500% YoY in 2023-2024
Hyperscaler capex arms race: $250B+ in 2024 alone
Magnificent 7 earnings growth far exceeded S&P 500 average
AI narrative drove broad sentiment shift
Soft landing achieved (no recession despite 525bp in hikes)

valuation_context.dot_com_comparison: Elevated but REAL earnings exist — not dot-com fiction

risks: DeepSeek shock Jan 2025: NVDA fell 17% in one day on low-cost Chinese AI model
Revenue ROI uncertainty: When do AI investments show customer-level returns?
Market concentration: Mag7 exit would drag S&P 500 heavily
Regulatory: EU AI Act, US chip export controls
Competition: Multiple well-funded AI foundation model companies
key_lesson: Technology adoption cycles can sustain elevated valuations far longer than skeptics expect IF earnings actually grow to justify them. NVDA's earnings grew faster than its stock price in 2023. The question is always: are earnings real and growing?
entry_signals: ChatGPT reaching 100M users in 60 days (fastest ever)
NVIDIA data center order backlog reports
Hyperscaler capex guidance upward revisions
AI revenue line items appearing in earnings reports
Fed pivot expectations building
note: Still ongoing as of 2026 — outcome unresolved

name: 1973–1974 Oil Crisis Bear Market
primary_causes: OPEC oil embargo October 1973 (US support for Israel in Yom Kippur War)
Oil price quadrupled from $3 to $12/barrel
Stagflation: High inflation + high unemployment simultaneously
Nixon closing gold window (August 1971) — dollar instability
Watergate political crisis (Nixon resigned August 1974)
Nixon wage and price controls distorted economy
Fed unable to fight inflation without causing deep recession
entry_signals: Yield curve inverted
Inflation rising (CPI >6%)
Oil prices rising before embargo
P/E ratios elevated heading into 1973
key_lesson: Stagflation is the hardest macro environment — neither expansionary nor contractionary policy works cleanly. Both equities and bonds can fail simultaneously.

name: 1980–1982 Volcker Double-Dip Recession
primary_causes: Fed Chair Volcker deliberately induced recession to break inflation
FFR raised to 20% (highest in US history)
Unemployment allowed to rise to 10.8%
Two recessions in 18 months
key_lesson: The cure for extreme inflation is painful recession. Volcker's willingness to cause pain broke the inflation psychology and set the stage for the 1983–2000 bull market.

name: 1998 LTCM / Russia Default Crisis
primary_causes: Russia defaulted on domestic debt August 17, 1998
LTCM had $1.25T in derivative positions on $5B equity (250:1 leverage)
LTCM's positions all correlated against them simultaneously
Credit spreads blew out globally
Emerging market contagion
policy_response: Fed organized 14-bank private bailout; cut rates 75bp in 3 moves (Sept-Nov 1998)
key_lesson: Leverage of 250:1 at a systemically important entity can create crisis even for non-recession environments. Speed of policy response determines recovery speed.

name: 2022 Inflation Bear Market
primary_causes: CPI reached 9.1% June 2022 (40-year high)
Fed behind the curve — still at 0% with CPI at 7%
Fastest rate hike cycle since 1980s (425bp in 9 months)
Multiple compression: high-growth stocks P/E collapsed
Speculative excess unwound: meme stocks, SPACs, crypto -70%+
Ukraine war (Feb 24, 2022) added commodity price shock
entry_signals: CAPE ratio ~38x (third highest ever) at January 2022
Fed still at 0% with CPI at 7% in January 2022
Yield curve rapidly flattening toward inversion
Record speculative excess: $700B SPAC issuance in 2021
Crypto, meme stocks, NFTs at peak
ARKK already down 40% from Nov 2021 (leading indicator of froth)
key_lesson: When Fed is behind the curve, the rapid catch-up tightening crushes growth multiples severely. Value stocks significantly outperform growth when rates normalize from extreme lows.

name: DJIA First Milestone Crossings
note: Each 10,000 level took progressively shorter time to reach from 1999–2024 as market size grew
